narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimated a 45% chance that you might fail the bar exam on your first attempt, which is high enough to raise concerns about your readiness. You have a strong family income background, plan to attend full-time like most applicants, and identify as male and Asian—traits that are not uncommon but underrepresented for Asian students compared to the mostly White dataset. However, your undergraduate GPA of 2.90 sits below the typical 3.21 average, your LSAT score of 27 is notably lower than the usual 36.70, and your school decile rankings—both at the bottom 10% for Year 1 and Year 3—are well below the middle-of-the-pack averages around 5.5 to 5.7. In assessing your profile, the model weighed your Year 3 decile ranking as the most influential factor, slightly nudging the prediction toward expecting a bar failure due to its low standing relative to peers. Right behind that was your LSAT score, which had the next strongest pull in the same concerning direction because of how far it trailed the norm. Your Year 1 decile followed closely as the third-biggest influence, reinforcing the risk signal from early academic struggles. Other elements like family income, racial background, undergraduate GPA, full-time status, and gender played smaller roles but still tipped the scales marginally toward failure in ranking order. Overall, these academic markers painted a picture riskier than the typical successful applicant, whose stronger grades and test scores align more closely with bar passers. To boost your future chances, focus on retaking the LSAT to aim for a score closer to or above 36—many students improve significantly with targeted prep. Building a stronger recent academic record, perhaps through postgraduate courses or certifications, could help offset those low deciles. While background factors like income or demographics aren't changeable, demonstrating commitment via full-time study and perhaps bar prep courses now will show growth. Reapplying after these steps could shift the model's view in your favor, as even small improvements in key areas like test scores make a big difference. Hang in there—many overcome early hurdles to succeed.
